requirements_for_qualification.summary: Must have earned income and meet deductibility requirements based on employer retirement plan coverage and income limits to make tax-deductible traditional IRA contributions reducing current taxable income while building retirement savings
criteria: Must have earned income (wages, self-employment, alimony from pre-2019 divorces)
2024 contribution limit: $7,000 under age 50; $8,000 age 50+ (catch-up contribution)
Full deduction if not covered by employer retirement plan regardless of income
If covered by employer plan: 2024 phase-out $77,000-$87,000 (single); $123,000-$143,000 (MFJ)
Spousal IRA: non-working spouse can contribute based on working spouse's income
Can contribute until tax filing deadline (typically April 15) for prior tax year
Deduction reduces AGI providing above-the-line tax benefit
Distributions in retirement taxed as ordinary income

traditional_ira_rules.contribution_limit: $7,000 ($8,000 if age 50+) for 2024; combined limit with Roth IRA
traditional_ira_rules.deductibility_full: Fully deductible if not covered by employer plan or income below phase-out
traditional_ira_rules.above_the_line: Deduction reduces AGI benefiting other AGI-based calculations
traditional_ira_rules.spousal_contribution: Working spouse's income enables non-working spouse contribution and deduction
traditional_ira_rules.flexibility_timing: Can contribute through tax filing deadline for prior year
traditional_ira_rules.tax_deferred_growth: Contributions and earnings grow tax-deferred until distributed

restrictions: Deductibility phases out if covered by employer plan and income exceeds thresholds
Combined $7,000/$8,000 limit applies to total of traditional and Roth IRA contributions
Cannot contribute more than earned income for the year
Early withdrawal before age 59½ subject to 10% penalty plus ordinary income tax (exceptions apply)
Required minimum distributions begin at age 73 (age 75 if born 1960+)
Excess contributions subject to 6% excise tax annually until corrected
Spousal IRA deductibility subject to separate phase-out ranges if spouse covered by employer plan
Pro-rata rule applies to distributions if mix of deductible and non-deductible contributions exists
